student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Conflict -- Pushing Back Without Burning Bridges
lesson.menu_desc: A German supplier missed three delivery milestones and now blames Motiva's late drawing approval. Tonight you learn to push back without burning bridges: the language of assertive conflict, the grammar that lets you concede a point and still hold your ground -- although, even though, while, whereas, despite -- and a full conflict-resolution call you run without escalating -- 42 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- Navigating Conflict
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 12 -- Navigating Conflict
lesson.grammar_point: concession and contrast clauses (although / even though / while / whereas / despite)

listenings.text: João, before the call with the supplier this afternoon, let me give you the picture, because the numbers matter more than the noise. GleisTech has missed three delivery milestones in a row, and this morning they sent a letter claiming the whole delay was caused by our late approval of the technical drawings. Now, part of that is true, and I want you to concede it openly. We were nine days late on one revision in January. But nine days does not explain a three-month slip, and the baseline schedule proves it. So here is what I need from you. Do not go in swinging, and do not roll over either. Acknowledge the drawing delay, because it is real and pretending otherwise costs us all our credibility. Then hold the line on the rest, with the schedule in front of you. We are not trying to win an argument this afternoon. We are trying to leave with a remediation plan and a supplier who still wants to work with us on the next project. Push back on the claim, not on the people. Find the common ground first, then draw the line where the evidence draws it.

Right, three things before you sit down for a conflict call, because engineers are usually either too soft or too hard, and both lose. First, separate the person from the problem. The moment you say you failed, the other side stops listening and starts defending, and you will never reach a remediation plan through a closed door. Say the schedule shows a gap, not you dropped the ball. It is the same fact, but one of them keeps the room open. Second, concede early and concede something real. If you were nine days late on a drawing, say so in the first two minutes. Giving ground on a small, true point is not weakness; it buys you enormous credibility when you hold firm on the big one. The engineer who admits nothing is never believed on anything. Third, always leave the other side a way to save face. Nobody signs a remediation plan that reads like a confession. Offer a path where they can commit to the fix without being publicly humiliated, and you will get the signature. Assertive is not aggressive. Assertive means you say the hard thing clearly, and you say it in a way the other person can still walk back from. Draw the line, but build the bridge across it at the same time.

patterns.title: Four ways to concede a point and still hold your ground
cards: Introduces a fact you accept, before the point you are really making. The main clause is where your weight goes. "Although we were late on one revision, that does not explain a three-month slip." Even though is the stronger, more emphatic version: "Even though I understand the pressure you were under, the contract is clear."

Sets your position beside theirs without attacking it. While can mean "although" or "at the same time as". Whereas draws a clean contrast between two facts. "While I accept the drawing was nine days late, the schedule shows the delay began earlier." "Our records show delivery in March, whereas the baseline called for January."
Concede with a phrase, not a clause

Followed by a NOUN or an -ing form, never a full clause with a verb. "Despite the late approval, we kept the line moving." "In spite of missing three milestones, they still want the contract." NOT "despite we were late" -- that needs although.

A full stop, then the pivot. However and nevertheless connect two separate sentences, not two clauses -- they take a comma after them. "We accept the drawing delay. However, the baseline schedule tells a different story." "The point is minor. Nevertheless, it is on record."

patterns.text: The trap is what comes AFTER the connector. although / even though / while / whereas take a full clause (subject + verb): "although WE WERE late". despite / in spite of take a noun or -ing: "despite THE DELAY", "despite BEING late" -- never "despite we were late". And however / nevertheless join two SENTENCES with a full stop before and a comma after. Same job -- concede and contrast -- three different grammars.

reading.rtitle: GleisTech Signaltechnik GmbH -- letter to Motiva, Ref. GT-2026-114 (adapted, written for João's package)
paras: Dear Mr. da Costa e Silva, we are writing in response to Motiva's notice of three missed delivery milestones on the interlocking supply for Line 4. While we do not dispute that the deliveries were late, we must place on record that, in our assessment, the primary cause lies with Motiva. The revised technical drawings were approved nine days after the date agreed in the baseline schedule, and our production line could not be released until that approval was received.

We understand Motiva's concern, and we share the objective of returning the project to its critical path. Nevertheless, we cannot accept sole responsibility for a delay that, in our view, began outside our control. Although we acknowledge that two of the three milestones slipped after the drawing approval was already in hand, we would ask Motiva to consider the cumulative effect of the initial delay on an already compressed schedule. In spite of these difficulties, GleisTech remains fully committed to the corrective action already under way, and we value the working relationship we have built over the past two years.

We therefore propose a joint remediation plan rather than an exchange of blame. We are prepared to commit additional shifts to recover the schedule, whereas a formal dispute would only delay both parties further. We would welcome a call this week to find common ground, agree a revised delivery sequence, and hold both sides accountable to it. We trust that, despite the current tension, this matter can be resolved in the spirit of partnership that has served the project well until now.
reading.source: GleisTech Signaltechnik GmbH -- formal correspondence, Ref. GT-2026-114 (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Notice how the supplier concedes to protect itself: "While we do not dispute that the deliveries were late...", "Although we acknowledge that two of the three milestones slipped...", "In spite of these difficulties...". Every concession is a bridge before a line. That is the exact move you learn to make tonight -- and to read, when it is made to you.

gist.prompt: What is GleisTech's letter really doing?
choices: Accepting full responsibility for all three missed milestones and offering to pay a penalty.
Conceding the deliveries were late while pushing the primary blame onto Motiva's drawing approval, and proposing a joint remediation plan to protect the relationship.
Refusing any further work and threatening to leave the project immediately.
Denying that any deliveries were late at all.

items: GleisTech denies that the deliveries were late.
f
The opposite. "While we do not dispute that the deliveries were late..." They concede the lateness openly -- and then use that concession to make their real argument about the cause.
GleisTech admits that two of the three milestones slipped after the drawings had already been approved.
t

"Although we acknowledge that two of the three milestones slipped after the drawing approval was already in hand..." It is a concession buried in an although-clause -- easy to miss, and the sentence you must push back on.
GleisTech is refusing to do any corrective work.
f
"...GleisTech remains fully committed to the corrective action already under way." They are signalling cooperation, not refusal -- which is exactly why an aggressive reply would be a mistake.
GleisTech would prefer a formal dispute to a negotiated plan.
f

"We therefore propose a joint remediation plan rather than an exchange of blame... whereas a formal dispute would only delay both parties further." They explicitly prefer the plan and want to preserve goodwill.
GleisTech wants both sides held accountable to a revised delivery sequence.
t
"...agree a revised delivery sequence, and hold both sides accountable to it." They accept mutual accountability -- your opening to concede the drawing delay and still hold the line on the rest.

items.situation: The supplier opens by blaming your late drawings for everything. Concede the true part first, then hold the line -- in one breath.
tips: Although we were nine days late on the January revision, and I accept that openly, the baseline schedule shows the remaining delay began on your side.
Let's separate the two: I'll own the drawing delay; whereas the two milestones after approval are ours to resolve together.

items.situation: They get defensive and stop listening. De-escalate without giving up your position.
tips: I'm not saying you dropped the ball -- I'm saying the schedule shows a gap, and I'd like us to close it together.
We're not here to win an argument. Despite the tension, we both want the same thing: the line back on its critical path.

items.situation: You've reached a sticking point on who pays for the recovery shifts. Draw the line, but keep the bridge.
tips: This is where I have to draw the line: the cost of the shifts after approval sits with GleisTech.
Even though we disagree on the cause, I'd rather meet you halfway on the schedule than lose a month to a formal dispute.

items.situation: They ask you to sign something that reads like a confession. Protect Motiva without burning the bridge.
tips: I can commit to a joint remediation plan; however, I can't sign a document that assigns Motiva sole responsibility.
Let's table that clause for now and agree the delivery sequence first, so nobody has to lose face to move forward.

items.situation: The call is heating up. Reset it toward common ground before it breaks.
tips: Can we pause the blame for a second and find the common ground? We both want Line 4 delivered and the partnership intact.
While the cause is still open, the fix isn't -- let's agree the recovery plan and hold both sides accountable to it.

items.situation: Close the call. Summarize what was agreed and keep the door open.
tips: So, to summarize: I've conceded the drawing delay, whereas the post-approval slip stays with your team, and we've agreed additional shifts to recover the schedule.
Despite a difficult start, I think we've smoothed things over -- I'll send the remediation plan today so we hold each other accountable.

bank.label: Conceding a point (although / even though / while)
items: Although we were nine days late on one revision, that does not explain a three-month slip.
Even though I understand the pressure your team was under, the baseline schedule is clear.
While I accept the drawing approval was late, the delay after it is ours to resolve together.

bank.label: Drawing a contrast (whereas / despite / in spite of)
items: Our records show delivery in March, whereas the baseline called for January.
Despite the late approval, we kept the installation line moving.
In spite of missing three milestones, GleisTech still wants the next contract -- so let's protect that.

bank.label: Pivoting firmly (however / nevertheless)
items: I can commit to a joint remediation plan. However, I can't sign a document that assigns us sole responsibility.
The drawing delay is real. Nevertheless, two of the three milestones slipped after approval.
We value the partnership. However, we have to hold both sides accountable to the revised sequence.

bank.label: Keeping the bridge (de-escalate, save face, common ground)
items: I'm not here to win an argument -- I'd rather meet you halfway and keep the line moving.
Let's table that clause for now and find the common ground first.
Despite the tension, I think we can smooth this over and leave with a plan we both signed.
